title: EastFlank Podcast #4: How the East Flank nations stand on Ukraine
content:
This time, senior editors from East Flank countries sit down to talk about military and humanitarian support for Ukraine, as well as how societies across the region have mobilised to help refugees.

As always, the conversation is inspired by our latest collaborative article – but we go beyond the text to share insights, perspectives, and behind-the-scenes context.

Tune in to the next episode of the East Flank Podcast. Don’t miss it!

The post EastFlank Podcast #4: How the East Flank nations stand on Ukraine appeared first on Eastflank.

title: Eastern Europe’s uneven front: How the East Flank nations stand on Ukraine
content:
Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022 upended the security balance of Europe and forced its eastern neighbors to confront hard questions about their own safety, alliances, and political identity.

The policies of Eastern Flank countries toward Ukraine reveal a complex landscape characterized by both solidarity and divergence. This report examines the positions of nine countries across NATO and the EU’s eastern flank – Bulgaria, Czechia, Finland, Hungary, Latvia, Lithuania, Romania, and Slovakia – toward Ukraine. All these countries share a border of anxiety and a history marked by Moscow’s shadow. But their responses to the war have not been the same. Some, like Poland and the Baltic states, treat Ukraine’s survival as inseparable from their own. Others, notably Hungary and Slovakia, have balked at sending weapons and adopted a cautious, even contrarian tone.

Military and non-military support: from strategic commitment to cautious assistance

The frontline supporters

Poland, Lithuania, Latv

title: The ‘peace camp’ Hungary and Slovakia are also profiting from the war, but the EU is no longer letting Orbán near EU defence funds
content:
Because of Russia’s war in Ukraine, the defence industry has taken off in both Slovakia and Hungary and, while Fico and Orbán are urging an end to support for Ukraine, both have requested money from the EU’s military build-up credit facility. Only one of them received any.

In the heat of the campaign, Hungarian prime minister Viktor Orbán called Shell and Erste Bank “the dogs of war” because two policy experts of the opposition Tisza Party, István Kapitány and András Kármán, previously worked for those companies. According to Orbán, these firms are profiteering from the war. Yet the sector that most clearly benefits from the war is the defence industry.

Although the annexation of the Crimean Peninsula in 2014 already foreshadowed that Russia would become the European Union’s main security challenge, most EU countries only started to spend more money on the defence industry after Moscow launched its war against Ukraine in February 2022.

The war in Ukraine represents a major opportunity for the arms industries of EU member states, and even the two countries that officially oppose supporting Ukraine are profiting from it. Hungary and S

title: Ukraine EU negotiations: Could it join next year?
content:
Hungary officially blocks the advancement of EU accession negotiations with Ukraine. Behind the scenes, however, much is happening, and Kyiv has high ambitions.

Every country’s accession negotiations with the European Union consist of six chapters, the so-called clusters. These include:

  • Fundamentals (fundamental rights, justice, freedom and security, public procurement, statistics, financial control, economic criteria, functioning of democratic institutions, and public administration reform),
  • Internal market (free movement of goods, freedom of movement for workers, right of establishment and freedom to provide services, free movement of capital, company law, intellectual property law, competition policy, financial services, consumer and health protection),
  • Competitiveness and inclusive growth (information society and media, taxation, economic and monetary policy, social policy and employment, enterprise and industrial policy, science and research, education and culture, customs union),
  • Green agenda and sustainab

title: “The great taking has begun.” Is Europe really planning to seize deposits?
content:
In recent weeks, a post by Ursula von der Leyen, President of the European Commission (EC), posted about a year ago, has gained popularity on the social media platform X. The post, which discusses the strategy for a savings and investments union, has been interpreted as a “great taking”.

In reality, the EC is speaking about voluntary investment opportunities, not the forced redirection of citizens’ deposits into investments or access to people’s bank accounts. The aim of the strategy is to create more opportunities for people, if they wish, to invest part of their savings in capital markets.

In her post, Ursula von der Leyen wrote: “Europe has all it needs to take the lead in the competitiveness race. Europe has everything it needs to become a leader in the race for competitiveness. This month the European Commission will unveil the EU Savings and Investments Union. We’ll turn private savings into much-needed investment. And we’ll work with institutional partners to get it off the ground.”

Users of the platform X have focused on this part of the post: “We’ll turn private savings into much-needed investmen

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: How the Epstein files shook Europe’s elites
content:
The release of Jeffrey Epstein’s files exposed the pathological ties of global elites and pushed public trust in those in power to the brink of collapse. In the shadows of global politics, a system had been operating beyond any real oversight.

When the U.S. Department of Justice finally released the long-awaited Jeffrey Epstein files, the world stood still. Across millions of pages of documents, emails, and meeting logs, the names of world leaders, business figures, and members of aristocracy began to surface.

The public reaction was immediate. Even the slightest mention in Epstein’s correspondence was enough to trigger waves of accusations, with crowds demanding political accountability  and – in many cases, heads to roll.

But this reaction goes beyond a simple desire for justice for the victims. It reflects a deeper, long-growing resentment toward global elites, who are increasingly seen by ordinary citizens as having monopolized power and drifted away from reality.

A Witch Hunt

Alongside authentic photos, documents, and emails revealin

title: There are no direct threats, but risks do exist. Energy infrastructure security is being strengthened
content:
Energy security in both Europe and the Baltic region has recently become one of the most strategically important issues.

This is also confirmed by the comprehensive strategy adopted by the European Commission (EC) in February to increase support for the eastern regions of the European Union (EU), in which the integration of the Baltic States’ electricity systems into European networks is cited as one of the key priorities.

The Baltic region has already invested significant effort and funding in improving its energy security, but work continues in several directions — strengthening electricity independence, ensuring price stability, and maintaining the continuity of power grids. The EC has approved substantial funding for a significant energy protection project to be implemented by the Baltic States and Poland.

A significant step has been taken

A year ago, the Baltic States connected to the European energy system, marking an important turning point in the region’s energy independence and the development of the sector, emphasises Līga Rozentāle, Deputy S

title: A desperate struggle for power: how Orban uses hatred towards Ukraine in his election campaign
content:
Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán, facing the threat of defeat in the parliamentary elections scheduled for April, cannot stop talking about Ukraine and its president, Volodymyr Zelenskyy. These conclusions were drawn in early March by the media outlet Kyiv Independent, and it is difficult to disagree with them.

As recently as early February, Viktor Orbán described the war-torn country as Hungary’s “enemy.” On the 7th of February, speaking at an election rally, the prime minister criticised Ukraine for its demand that the European Union (EU) halt imports of cheap Russian energy: “Anyone who says such a thing is Hungary’s enemy; therefore, Ukraine is our enemy.”

These comments came shortly after the EU Council approved plans to completely ban Russian gas imports by the end of 2027. Complaints were voiced not only by Hungary but also by Slovakia, which is similarly dependent on Moscow’s resources, with Budapest even taking the matter to the EU court.

The issue of Russian energy supplies is further complicated by the conflict in the Middle East, which has triggered the highest rise in energy pri

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Will Latvia seek shelter under France’s “nuclear umbrella”?
content:
At the beginning of the year, as the United States intensified its ambition to take control of Greenland, European leaders actively debated whether Europe itself needs its own “nuclear umbrella”. At the Munich Security Conference, Latvia’s Prime Minister Evika Siliņa (“New Unity”) told Politico: “Why not?”

Now, France — the only nuclear-armed country in the European Union — has announced a new nuclear doctrine and listed the countries with which it will conduct joint nuclear military exercises. Latvia is not on France’s list. Why? “The choice of specific countries for joint military exercises with France is decided by France itself. That does not mean that opportunities for cooperation with other countries do not exist,” Siliņa explained to Delfi.

According to Politico, on the 2nd of March, French President Emmanuel Macron delivered an important speech in which he announced that France would increase the number of nuclear weapons at its disposal and would no longer publicly disclose their exact number. “One of our submarines carries as much striking power as all the bombs dropped in Europ

title: Support for Ukraine by EU/NATO Eastern Flank Countries
content:
The infographic presents data showing how nine countries on the EU and NATO’s eastern flank support Ukraine.

It clearly highlights significant differences in the scale of engagement—while some countries treat support for Ukraine as a component of their own security, others limit their assistance to a minimum. The graphic is part of the East Flank report, which provides a detailed analysis of the level and nature of support given to Ukraine by countries in the region
